scope_and_product_coverage.description: Which hardware and software products fall within the regulation's mandatory requirements, including debates over open-source software, SaaS, and bespoke enterprise solutions.
scope_and_product_coverage.salience: Tech industry lobbies hard to narrow scope and exclude open-source/SaaS; civil society and ENISA push for broad coverage to close gaps; SMEs fear disproportionate compliance burden.

essential_cybersecurity_requirements.description: The stringency and prescriptiveness of the mandatory security-by-design and security-by-default technical requirements manufacturers must meet.
essential_cybersecurity_requirements.salience: Manufacturers want flexible, principles-based requirements; security researchers and consumer groups demand concrete, enforceable standards; tension between innovation and prescriptive mandates.

support_period_and_lifecycle_obligations.description: How long manufacturers must provide security updates and support for products, and what obligations apply at end-of-life.
support_period_and_lifecycle_obligations.salience: Consumer and civil society groups demand long support periods; hardware manufacturers resist open-ended obligations; debate over who bears cost when products outlive commercial support windows.

conformity_assessment_and_third_party_ve.description: Whether self-declaration of conformity is sufficient or mandatory third-party assessment by notified bodies is required, and for which product risk classes.
conformity_assessment_and_third_party_ve.salience: Industry prefers self-declaration to reduce cost and speed time-to-market; regulators and security experts push for mandatory third-party assessment for high-risk products; notified bodies lobby for expanded mandates.

vulnerability_disclosure_and_incident_re.description: Mandatory timelines and mechanisms for reporting actively exploited vulnerabilities and security incidents to ENISA and market surveillance authorities.
vulnerability_disclosure_and_incident_re.salience: Security researchers and vendors clash over mandatory 72-hour reporting windows that may force premature disclosure before patches are ready; intelligence agencies concerned about mandatory reporting creating exploitable intelligence; tension with NIS2 reporting obligations.

open_source_software_treatment.description: Whether and how open-source software projects and their stewards bear obligations under the regulation, including exemptions for non-commercial development.
open_source_software_treatment.salience: Open-source foundations and community developers fear being liable for widely-used projects they maintain voluntarily; commercial vendors that build on OSS want clarity on who is responsible; Commission seeks not to chill open-source innovation while closing supply-chain gaps.

market_surveillance_and_enforcement.description: How market surveillance authorities across Member States coordinate enforcement, including powers to withdraw non-compliant products and impose penalties.
market_surveillance_and_enforcement.salience: Member States with lighter regulatory traditions resist strong Commission oversight; industry warns of fragmented enforcement creating unlevel playing field; debate over penalty caps (up to €15M or 2.5% global turnover) and their proportionality for SMEs.

sme_and_proportionality.description: Whether the regulation's obligations are proportionate for small and medium-sized enterprises and micro-enterprises, and what targeted relief or phase-in periods apply.
sme_and_proportionality.salience: SME associations and startup lobbies argue compliance costs are existential for small players; consumer groups counter that exemptions create dangerous loopholes; Commission seeks balance between innovation and security floors.

standardisation_and_harmonised_standards.description: Which technical standards satisfy the essential requirements, who develops them, and the role of European Standardisation Organisations versus international bodies.
standardisation_and_harmonised_standards.salience: Industry wants to use existing international standards (ISO/IEC) rather than bespoke EU standards; ESOs and Commission want EU standards to reflect European values and policy goals; debate over presumption of conformity timelines.

supply_chain_security_obligations.description: The extent to which manufacturers must assess and take responsibility for the cybersecurity of third-party components and upstream software dependencies.
supply_chain_security_obligations.salience: Large integrators fear liability for vulnerabilities in components they did not develop; component vendors resist obligations flowing down; security community and government users demand SBOMs for critical infrastructure procurement decisions.

interface_with_sectoral_legislation.description: How the Cyber Resilience Act interacts with and avoids duplication or conflict with sector-specific regulations such as NIS2, the AI Act, the Radio Equipment Directive, and the Medical Devices Regulation.
interface_with_sectoral_legislation.salience: Regulated industries (health, automotive, aviation) lobby to stay under their existing sectoral regimes; Commission faces political pressure to ensure coherence; lex specialis carve-outs create fragmentation risks.

essential_cybersecurity_requirements.description: The stringency and prescriptiveness of the mandatory security-by-design and security-by-default technical requirements manufacturers must meet.

support_period_and_lifecycle_obligations.description: How long manufacturers must provide security updates and support for products, and what obligations apply at end-of-life.

conformity_assessment_and_third_party_ve.description: Whether self-declaration of conformity is sufficient or mandatory third-party assessment by notified bodies is required, and for which product risk classes.

vulnerability_disclosure_and_incident_re.description: Mandatory timelines and mechanisms for reporting actively exploited vulnerabilities and security incidents to ENISA and market surveillance authorities.

market_surveillance_and_enforcement.description: How market surveillance authorities across Member States coordinate enforcement, including powers to withdraw non-compliant products and impose penalties.

positions.summary: OVH highlighted concerns about the European cloud market being dominated by non-European actors and raised issues of data protection and extraterritoriality, without taking an explicit position on the CRA proposal.

OVH highlighted concerns about the European cloud market being dominated by non-European actors and raised issues of data protection and extraterritoriality, without taking an explicit position on the CRA proposal.

JPMorgan reported on their experience implementing CRA and DORA cybersecurity requirements and expressed interest in simplification of digital regulatory obligations, signalling a preference for reduced compliance burden.

orgs: European Association of Paritarian Institutions of Social Protection
positions.summary: Assofondipensione addressed DORA implications in the context of pension institutions alongside PEPP, without articulating a clear position on modifying the CRA proposal.

PwC called for harmonisation of cloud-related regulations across the EU, arguing that conflicts between national rules and EU schemes such as DORA and EUCS create barriers to cross-border solutions and should be resolved through a converging regulatory path.

orgs: European Association of Public Banks and Funding agencies AISBL
positions.summary: EAPB advocated for simplification and proportionality of the promotional banks' regulatory framework, raising concerns about cumulative compliance burdens from DORA alongside other reporting and disclosure obligations.

The European Cloud User Coalition supported integrating DORA into a unified cybersecurity incident reporting platform, endorsing streamlined interoperability between DORA and broader cybersecurity reporting obligations.

Cisco engaged with the Commission on its security agenda, including NIS2 and Cyber Resilience Act implementation and the upcoming review of the Cybersecurity Act, without articulating a specific position on the CRA's core provisions.

Commerzbank calls for a pragmatic, use-case-focused simplification of AI regulation and a clearer, less duplicative interplay between DORA and the Cyber Resilience Act to reduce compliance burden on the banking sector.

DIHK raises concerns about the cumulative implementation burden of NIS2, DORA, and the Cyber Resilience Act on small companies, calling for proportionate certification requirements and a more workable cybersecurity acquis.

Palo Alto Networks discussed implementation of NIS2, DORA, and the Cyber Resilience Act alongside personal data use in cybersecurity and the future of ePrivacy, without signalling a clear supporting or opposing position.

Verisure expresses concern about the Cyber Resilience Act's implementation timeline and its revenue and product impacts across the digital ecosystem, while also questioning AI Act transparency obligations and standardisation processes.

orgs: European Association of Public Banks and Funding agencies AISBL
positions.summary: EAPB focused exclusively on financial services legislation (DORA, FIDA, EMIR, MiFIR, SFTR, CRR/CRD) and raised no positions relevant to the Cyber Resilience Act taxonomy.

Amadeus IT Group called for reducing the combined regulatory burden of NIS2, the Cyber Resilience Act, GDPR, and AI legislation through a digital simplification agenda, highlighting the cumulative compliance pressure across overlapping frameworks.

orgs: International Federation of Organic Agriculture Movements EU Regional Group
positions.summary: NGO coalition focused entirely on pesticide phase-out and agricultural authorisation frameworks, raising no positions relevant to the Cyber Resilience Act taxonomy.
EuroCommerce discussed food chain benchmarking and unfair trading practices regulation in an agricultural policy forum, raising no positions relevant to the Cyber Resilience Act taxonomy.

orgs: European Association of Paritarian Institutions of Social Protection
positions.summary: Social protection institutions raised concerns about DORA's interaction with pension and complementary social protection frameworks, with no clear position articulated on the CRA proposal itself.

The European Banking Federation sought modifications to the CRA's standardisation approach and called for clearer delineation of its scope relative to DORA to avoid duplicative obligations on financial sector entities.

The European Banking Federation argued that CRA incident reporting and product security requirements should be aligned with or defer to DORA obligations already applicable to financial institutions, to prevent regulatory duplication.

Kyndryl raised issues around cybersecurity certification processes and the interplay with DORA, alongside concerns about cybersecurity skills availability, without taking an explicit position for or against the CRA proposal.
Apple opposes the CRA's notification obligations, reiterating a critical position on mandatory incident reporting requirements and lawful data access provisions.
Broadcom raised CRA implementation issues including conformity assessment, but the meeting notes do not specify a clear position for or against the Commission proposal.

The Austrian Chamber of Commerce raised concerns about the cumulative cybersecurity regulatory burden on small companies, implicitly calling for proportionality adjustments in the CRA and related instruments like NIS2.

The German public banks association calls for harmonising and streamlining overlapping reporting obligations across DORA, NIS2, and the CRA, demanding proportionality and risk-based approaches to reduce burdens on smaller financial players.
Broadcom engaged on CRA implementation broadly alongside cloud sovereignty and data protection, but no specific position or modification was recorded.

Squareup called for a more coherent and predictable legal framework, citing contradictory requirements between the Batteries Recycling Directive and the Cyber Resilience Act as an example of problematic regulatory overlap.

pair_details.reasoning: OVH took no explicit position on the CRA proposal, so this amendment on substantial modification liability is neither aligned nor opposed to a stated stance.
OVH took no explicit position on the CRA proposal, so this amendment on substantial modification liability is neither aligned nor opposed to a stated stance.
No specific Broadcom position on scope or product coverage was recorded, making it impossible to assess directional alignment with this amendment.
Designating ENISA as the central hub for vulnerability and incident notifications directly supports the coalition's goal of a unified, interoperable cybersecurity reporting platform.
Switching from implementing to delegated acts for notification format is a procedural change that does not materially advance or hinder DORA-CRA interoperability.
Expanding the scope of delegated acts to cover additional reporting articles is procedurally neutral with only indirect and uncertain effects on DORA integration.
The revocation provision mirrors amendment 437 and is a governance procedural matter with no direct bearing on DORA-CRA reporting alignment.

Establishing a separate ENISA-based CRA notification channel without any carve-out for DORA-regulated financial institutions creates exactly the parallel reporting duplication the EBF seeks to prevent.
The switch to delegated acts could theoretically allow the Commission to harmonise CRA notifications with DORA, but the amendment itself provides no such guarantee, making the net effect neutral.
Broadening the Commission's delegated powers over reporting articles is procedurally neutral and does not itself reduce DORA-CRA overlap.
Revocation provisions for delegated acts are a governance formality with no substantive impact on regulatory alignment.
Confirming ENISA as the mandatory recipient of vulnerability and incident notifications directly entrenches the mandatory reporting obligations Apple opposes.
Changing from implementing to delegated acts alters the legislative procedure for specifying notification details but does not reduce or eliminate the underlying reporting obligation.
Extending delegated act powers to additional reporting articles is procedurally neutral and does not materially change Apple's exposure to mandatory notification requirements.
The expanded revocation clause is a procedural governance provision with no direct effect on the notification obligations Apple opposes.
Adding an ENISA-based CRA reporting channel without streamlining or deferring to existing DORA/NIS2 obligations increases overlapping burdens rather than reducing them.
The shift to delegated acts for notification format could in principle enable harmonised cross-framework requirements, but this is speculative and the amendment itself is procedurally neutral.
Expanding delegated act coverage to more reporting articles is procedural and does not itself deliver the proportionality or streamlining the association demands.
The revocation provision is a governance formality with no substantive effect on reporting harmonisation or proportionality.

scope_and_product_coverage.summary: The scope remained broad across all stages, covering hardware and software products with digital elements, but the final text expanded the list of amended legal instruments (adding Regulation (EU) No 168/2013 on motor vehicles and Directive (EU) 2020/1828) and the act was given an official name and number (Cyber Resilience Act, (EU) 2024/2847), reflecting refinements in boundary-setting with adjacent sectoral legislation. The committee stage introduced more explicit framing around 'essential cybersecurity requirements' and a 'uniform legal framework,' signalling a consolidation of scope rather than a narrowing, while the final adopted text also added a joint institutional statement on ENISA resources, indicating that enforcement capacity was recognised as integral to the regulation's effective product coverage.

stage_notes.note: Identified a gap in EU law for non-embedded software and most hardware/software products not covered by existing legislation; proposed broad horizontal scope covering all products with digital elements placed on the internal market, citing cross-border risk and high-profile incidents (WannaCry, Kaseya) as justification.

Retained the broad horizontal scope but sharpened the language around 'essential cybersecurity requirements' and a 'uniform legal framework'; the title expanded to include Directive 2020/1828/EC as an amended act, signalling early-stage negotiation on boundaries with consumer-protection legislation and implying scope clarifications were being negotiated.

Final adopted text further expanded the list of co-amended instruments to include Regulation (EU) No 168/2013 (motor vehicles/machinery), reflecting boundary negotiations with sectoral legislation; the act received its official designation (EU) 2024/2847 and a joint institutional statement on ENISA resources was appended, acknowledging that adequate enforcement infrastructure is necessary to make the product scope operationally effective.

essential_cybersecurity_requirements.summary: The provisions evolved from a problem-framing context in the Commission Proposal into a formal, binding legal framework in the Committee Report, explicitly mandating essential cybersecurity requirements as a uniform standard for market placement and extending obligations to the full product lifecycle. The Text Adopted confirms Parliament's consolidated first-reading position, indicating the strengthened lifecycle and security-by-design requirements survived trilogue and were codified.

stage_notes.note: Provisions are framed primarily as justification and problem diagnosis — highlighting widespread vulnerabilities, inconsistent security updates, and user information gaps. No specific mandatory technical requirements are articulated in the excerpt; the focus is on market failure rationale rather than prescriptive obligations.

Language shifts to formal legal obligation: 'a uniform legal framework for essential cybersecurity requirements' is explicitly established. The Committee adds that manufacturers must 'take security seriously throughout a product's life cycle', introducing a lifecycle obligation not present in the Commission Proposal excerpt. Security-by-design and support-period transparency are referenced as concrete dimensions of the requirements.

The procedural resolution text confirms Parliament adopted its first-reading position based on the Committee report (A9-0253/2023) and that a provisional trilogue agreement was reached. This indicates the strengthened essential cybersecurity requirements and lifecycle obligations from the Committee stage were maintained in the final adopted text.

support_period_and_lifecycle_obligations.summary: The theme evolved from a general problem statement about insufficient security update provision in the Commission Proposal to an explicit regulatory objective of ensuring manufacturers take security seriously throughout a product's lifecycle and improving transparency regarding support periods. The Committee Report and final Text Adopted codified lifecycle obligations and support period transparency as concrete regulatory aims, elevating informal concerns into binding framework requirements.

stage_notes.content: Identifies insufficient and inconsistent provision of security updates as a major problem, framed as motivation for the proposal but without specific lifecycle or support period obligations.

stage_notes.note: Explicitly introduces 'support period' as a transparency requirement and mandates that manufacturers take security seriously 'throughout a product's life cycle', converting the problem statement into a concrete regulatory objective.

Adopts the Parliament's position incorporating the committee's strengthened lifecycle and support period framework, confirmed via trilogue agreement with the Council, embedding these obligations into the final legislative text.

conformity_assessment_and_third_party_ve.summary: The Commission Proposal established a tiered conformity assessment regime where most products could use self-declaration while Class I and Class II critical products faced escalating third-party requirements; the Committee Report reinforced proportionality and sector-specific risk calibration, and the final adopted text refined product classifications and expanded the circumstances under which notified-body involvement is mandatory, without abandoning the self-assessment route for lower-risk products.

stage_notes.note: Introduced a risk-based classification distinguishing default products (self-declaration via internal control), Class I critical products (self-declaration permitted but harmonised standards or ENISA schemes encouraged), and Class II critical products (mandatory third-party assessment by a notified body), reflecting a graduated approach tied to the product's criticality.

The ITRE committee emphasised proportionality and the need for Commission guidelines clarifying classification criteria; it pushed to ensure that the criticality thresholds—and thus the trigger for mandatory notified-body assessment—were clearly defined and sector-sensitive, signalling pressure to expand or clarify Class I/II product lists without fully abandoning self-declaration for lower-risk categories.

The Parliament's first-reading position, reflecting the trilogues' provisional agreement, retained the tiered structure but refined product annexes and introduced clearer conditions under which manufacturers of important and critical products must engage notified bodies, strengthening third-party oversight compared to the original proposal while preserving self-declaration for the broad base of non-critical products with digital elements.

vulnerability_disclosure_and_incident_re.summary: The Commission Proposal identified a gap in mandatory cybersecurity requirements for products with digital elements and referenced NIS2 as the existing incident-reporting baseline, but did not itself specify vulnerability disclosure timelines. By the Text Adopted stage (Regulation EU 2024/2847), the institutional infrastructure supporting enforcement of those obligations was explicitly reinforced through a joint political statement committing to increase ENISA's human resources and expertise to handle the additional workload created by the new reporting obligations.

stage_notes.note: Frames the legislative gap: existing EU law (NIS2, Cybersecurity Act) covers services and certification but lacks mandatory security requirements—including vulnerability and incident reporting—for products with digital elements. No specific timelines for vulnerability disclosure are visible in the provided excerpt.

The committee moved from gap identification to concrete institutional backing, formally requesting 9 additional ENISA full-time posts and corresponding appropriations to ensure ENISA can fulfil its new obligations under the regulation without compromising existing duties.

A joint political statement by Parliament, Council, and Commission was appended to the final Regulation (EU) 2024/2847, acknowledging that the Act confers additional tasks on ENISA and committing the Commission to assess ENISA resource needs—particularly human resources with adequate cybersecurity expertise—in the first budgetary cycle after entry into force, thereby institutionally anchoring the enforcement capacity for vulnerability disclosure and incident reporting obligations.

open_source_software_treatment.summary: The Commission Proposal made no explicit mention of open-source software or exemptions for non-commercial development in the provided text, treating all products with digital elements uniformly under cybersecurity requirements. By the Committee Report stage, open-source was explicitly acknowledged as a sector requiring tailored guidance, with Recital 4a calling on the Commission to issue detailed guidelines covering the implications of the Regulation for free and open-source developers, signalling a move toward proportionality and recognition of OSS specificity.

stage_notes.note: No explicit mention of open-source software or exemptions for non-commercial development in the provided text; the proposal frames obligations broadly for all products with digital elements without differentiating OSS stewards.

Recital 4a explicitly names 'free and open-source developers' as a category requiring clarification, directing the Commission to issue guidelines on the scope's implications for OSS; this reflects lobbying pressure to acknowledge OSS distinctions and introduce proportionality.

The final adopted text references a provisional agreement reached in December 2023, which in the broader CRA context is known to include dedicated OSS exemptions for non-commercial development and stewardship roles; the provided excerpt does not reproduce the operative articles, but the adoption of the trilogue agreement implies these OSS-specific provisions were retained.

market_surveillance_and_enforcement.summary: The Commission Proposal identified a regulatory gap for mandatory cybersecurity requirements for products with digital elements and implicitly relied on existing market surveillance frameworks; by the Text Adopted stage, the regulation explicitly amends Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 (the Market Surveillance Regulation), formally embedding coordinated enforcement powers—including product withdrawal and penalties—into the CRA's legal architecture. The Committee Report stage served as the bridge, with rapporteurs integrating market surveillance alignment into the text, though the provided excerpts do not reveal the granular amendment language itself.

stage_notes.note: The excerpt focuses on identifying gaps in existing EU cybersecurity legislation (NIS, NIS2, Cybersecurity Act) regarding mandatory product requirements. Market surveillance is not explicitly addressed in the provided text; enforcement is implicitly assumed to follow existing horizontal frameworks without specifying coordination mechanisms or penalty powers.

The report header confirms the proposal's scope includes amending Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 (the EU Market Surveillance Regulation), signalling that the committee actively worked to integrate CRA enforcement into the existing market surveillance architecture. The associated committee (IMCO) role suggests scrutiny of internal market and consumer protection enforcement angles.

The adopted text formally confirms the regulation amends Regulation (EU) 2019/1020, institutionalising market surveillance authorities' powers to oversee CRA compliance. The provisional agreement with the Council (December 2023) indicates that enforcement coordination, product withdrawal powers, and penalties were negotiated and consolidated into the final text, representing a more structured and explicit enforcement regime than the original proposal.

sme_and_proportionality.summary: The Commission Proposal made only a generic reference to proportionality under Article 114 TFEU without addressing SMEs specifically, while the Committee Report explicitly introduced language on harmonisation and proportionality for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises as a core rationale. By the Text Adopted stage, the provisional agreement reflected the committee's reinforced framing, embedding SME-focused proportionality as an explicit legislative objective rather than a procedural footnote.

stage_notes.note: No specific SME provisions visible; proportionality referenced only in the context of the legal basis (Article 114 TFEU) without targeted relief or phase-in measures for SMEs.

Explicit and repeated references to micro, small and medium-sized enterprises added; proportionality for SMEs framed as a key justification for Union-level harmonisation, with language on reducing unnecessary burden and creating viable market-entry conditions.

The final resolution reflects the provisional agreement reached after trilogues, which incorporated the committee's SME-proportionality language; the procedural text confirms Parliament's position endorsing those reinforced SME protections.

stage_notes.note: Detailed provisions on standardisation: explicit reference to ESOs, harmonised standards development, standardisation request (Commission Implementing Decision C(2022)5637), planned repeal/amendment of Delegated Regulation 2022/30 to avoid overlap, and a coordination clause to prevent duplication of standardisation work between the Commission and ESOs.

By the Committee Report stage, the framing shifted toward identifying the explicit legislative gap—acknowledging that existing Union law addresses supply chain security only partially and does not impose mandatory product-level requirements—setting the stage for such obligations to be codified, though the final adopted text excerpt provided is procedural and does not reveal the operative articles that would confirm whether those obligations were ultimately strengthened or diluted.

This sharpens the legislative gap and signals intent to introduce binding obligations, implying stronger supply chain accountability for manufacturers.
